Getting Started · Automai Watcher

Get Started with Automai Watcher

In this guide you will install Automai, record a real Salesforce login scenario, and set up a bot that automatically monitors it every 5 minutes — alerting you the moment it fails.

What you will have by the end

A live process monitor checking your Salesforce login every 5 minutes, running automatically on a schedule, with a real bot executing the scenario on its own desktop — no manual intervention needed.

Windows Server required Salesforce account needed Administrator access needed
Install
1 Download & install 2 Log in to Director 3 Verify BotManager
Record
4 Record the scenario 5 Send to Director
Configure & Launch
6 Add rWatcher 7 Create a schedule 8 Create Process Monitor 9 See it run 10 Export reports
1
Step 1

Download & install Automai

The Automai suite installs everything in one go — Director, BotManager, and ScenarioBuilder. Download the Watcher installer and run it on your Windows Server.

→ Download Automai Watcher installer

Run as Administrator

Right-click the installer and select Run as administrator. The installation will not complete correctly without administrator privileges.

Follow the installation wizard

1

Select COMPLETE INSTALL for all modules — already selected by default and labelled "Recommended for Evaluation". Click Next.

Choose COMPLETE INSTALL — pre-selected and recommended
2

The Web Server Configuration screen appears. Leave the port as 8888 and the path as C:\Automai. Click Next.

Web Server Configuration — leave port 8888 as default
3

The Launcher User Information screen appears with your hostname and port 8888 already filled in. Click Next.

Launcher User Information — auto-populated, just click Next
4

Installation begins. Wait for the progress bar to complete — this takes a few minutes.

Installation in progress
5

When you see "Completing the AutomaiSuite Setup Wizard", click Finish. Nothing opens automatically.

Setup complete — click Finish
6

Two shortcuts appear on your desktop: Automai Director and ScenarioBuilder. You will use both in this guide.

Desktop shortcuts created after installation
2
Step 2

Log in to Director

Double-click the Automai Director shortcut. It opens your browser automatically and navigates to Director — no URL to type.

Your browser may show "Not secure" — this is expected for a local installation. You can proceed safely.

1

Enter the default credentials — Username: admin / Password: automai — and click Login.

Director login page — default credentials shown at the bottom
2

Director opens to Automai Watcher. You will see an empty dashboard — this is expected. No monitoring has been configured yet.

Change your password after first login from the admin menu in the top right corner.

3
Step 3

Verify BotManager is connected

BotManager was installed and connected automatically. You just need to confirm it is showing as connected before continuing.

1

In the top navigation, click rWatchers. Then click the BotManagers sub-tab.

2

You should see BotManager - 1 with a green checkmark confirming it is connected and ready.

BotManagers tab — BotManager-1 connected with green checkmark
BotManager not showing?

Confirm that port 8888 is open in your Windows Firewall and that no antivirus software is blocking the connection.

Troubleshooting BotManager issues

Bots disconnecting from Director during test execution

If bots disconnect during a test run, the cause is usually one of two things:

Script-related disconnect — happens only during specific test runs. Check CPU and memory usage on the BotManager server during the test. If resource usage is high, try running the test with fewer bots or with a simpler script (e.g. open Notepad, wait 20 seconds, close). If the bots don't disconnect with a simpler script, the issue is in your scenario. Adding a Funnel Point action to the part of the script that restarts the application after a failure can help control how many bots execute those steps simultaneously.

Script download disconnect — all bots on a BotManager disconnect before the test even starts. This means the BotManager is spending too long downloading scripts. Fix: uncheck the Send Scenarios option in the start test run window.

→ Full article: Bots disconnect from Director randomly

Bots not reconnecting after a server restart or downtime

After restarting your server, bots may fail to reconnect automatically. To fix this:

  1. Go to the rDesktopManager launcher machine and navigate to the BotManager/bots folder.
  2. Locate the remote bot's RDP file. The path follows this structure: pcname/Watchers/username/username.rdp
  3. Open the .rdp file in a text editor and find the line AutoReconnectionEnabled:s:1. Update it to autoreconnection enabled:s:1.
  4. Save the file and restart the BotManager service.
  5. Verify bots reconnect by checking their status in rDesktopManager.

→ Full article: Reconnect my bot after restart or downtime

4
Step 4

Record the Salesforce login scenario

ScenarioBuilder is code-free — recording is as easy as using your application normally. The engine analyzes the desktop and acts on specific images or changes it sees on screen. There is no programming involved.

Do this manually first

Before recording, go through the Salesforce login process yourself: open Salesforce → enter username → enter password → click Log In → confirm home page loads → click avatar → Log Out. Know every step before you record it.

Open ScenarioBuilder

1

Double-click the ScenarioBuilder shortcut on your desktop. A loading screen appears — wait a few seconds for it to finish.

ScenarioBuilder loading screen
2

ScenarioBuilder opens directly to the New Scenario form. Enter Salesforce login in the Name field and leave Type as Process Scenario.

New Scenario form — opens automatically on first launch
3

Since Salesforce is a web application, click Open web app.

4

The Open URL dialog appears. Select your browser and enter your Salesforce URL. Click OK.

Open URL dialog — select browser and enter Salesforce URL
5

Your browser opens to the Salesforce login page and the Recording ON bar appears in the bottom right corner. ScenarioBuilder is now capturing everything you do.

Recording in progress — Recording ON bar visible bottom right
6

Go through the Salesforce login process exactly as you practised:

  • Enter your Salesforce username
  • Enter your password
  • Click Log In
  • Wait for the Salesforce home page to fully load before doing anything else
  • Click your avatar in the top right and select Log Out

Keep the steps simple — login and logout is enough. The bot will repeat this exact flow every 5 minutes to confirm your application is responding correctly.

7

Click the Stop button in the Recording ON toolbar. ScenarioBuilder returns to the main window with your recorded steps listed as individual actions.

ScenarioBuilder — the recorded Salesforce login script showing all steps

Validate the scenario

1

In the ScenarioBuilder toolbar, click Play to run the scenario back from the start.

ScenarioBuilder toolbar — Play button in the Run group
2

If the playback completes without errors, the scenario is valid. Run it 2–3 times to confirm it is consistent before sending to Director.

If the scenario fails during playback, check for browser popups that appeared during recording — like "Save your password?" — that were not there when you practised manually. Disable browser password saving in your browser settings, then re-record.

Troubleshooting — scenario playback failures

Having issues? See: Troubleshooting failures in a scenario

Having issues? See: How to fix image failures using Smart Image Configurator

5
Step 5

Send the scenario to Director

Once the scenario is validated, send it to Director so it can be assigned to a Process Monitor.

1

In the ScenarioBuilder toolbar, click Send Scenario in the File group on the left.

Send Scenario button — File group, left of toolbar
2

The Director Credentials dialog appears. Enter admin and your Director password, then click Login.

Director Credentials dialog
3

The Script Version dialog appears. Leave all defaults — Minor is pre-selected, version set to 0.001. Add an optional comment, then click Send Script.

Script Version dialog — leave defaults, click Send Script
4

A Sent Successfully confirmation appears. Click OK.

Sent Successfully confirmation
5

Go back to Director in your browser. Click Projects in the top navigation. Confirm your Salesforce login process appears and the Project is open toggle is enabled.

Projects page — Salesforce login arrived, Project is open

The project name shown is Test — this is the default project ScenarioBuilder created automatically. The scenario inside it is called Salesforce login. You will select both when creating the Process Monitor.

6
Step 6

Add an rWatcher

An rWatcher is the bot that will run your Salesforce login scenario on schedule. Create one and start it before creating a Process Monitor.

1

Click rWatchers in the top navigation. The list is empty — click Add new rWatcher.

rWatchers tab — empty list, Add new rWatcher button visible
2

Fill in the Add new rWatcher form:

  • Select BotManager — choose BotManager - 1
  • New rWatcher username — e.g. rWatcher01
  • rWatcher password — minimum 6 characters
  • New rWatcher alias — e.g. rWatcher-Salesforce-01
  • RDP parameters — leave defaults

Click Submit.

Add new rWatcher form — filled in for Salesforce monitoring
3

The rWatcher appears with a Registered status. It is registered but not yet active.

rWatcher registered — needs to be started
4

Select the rWatcher using the checkbox, then click Start rWatchers.

Click Start rWatchers to activate the bot
5

Wait up to 2 minutes, then click the refresh button. The rWatcher is ready when the computer icon on the left side of the row turns green.

rWatcher available — green computer icon on the left confirms it is ready

Having issues? See: My rWatchers in Director UI are not starting

Do not proceed until the icon is green

Creating a Process Monitor before the rWatcher is available will result in the monitor not running.

Troubleshooting — BotManager connection issues

Having issues? See: Bots disconnect from Director randomly

Having issues? See: Reconnect my bot after restart or downtime

7
Step 7

Create a schedule

A schedule tells Automai Watcher how often to run your Process Monitor. You will create a 5-minute interval so you see results quickly.

1

Click Schedules in the top navigation → click the Schedules sub-tab → click + Schedule.

Schedules page — click + Schedule
2

Fill in the form:

  • Schedule Name — enter 5 mins
  • Schedule Type — leave as Interval
  • Interval Value — set to 5, Unit: Minutes
  • Active Days — leave all days selected

Click Save.

New Schedule form — 5 minute interval, all days active
3

The schedule appears with a blue active toggle. The Next run date shows N/A — this is expected and will populate once assigned to a Process Monitor.

Schedule created and active

Optional — create a maintenance calendar

Calendars let you define exclusion periods — date and time ranges when your schedule should not run. Use them to pause monitoring during public holidays, planned maintenance windows, or company events. Once created, a calendar can be assigned to any schedule.

1

Click the Calendars sub-tab under Schedules, then click + Calendar.

Calendars tab — click + Calendar to create a new exclusion calendar
2

Fill in the Calendar information:

  • Calendar Name — a unique name e.g. April Weekends
  • Description — optional, e.g. Pause monitoring on weekends
  • Timezone — set your timezone

Under Calendar Dates, Days & Hours, click + New to add an exclusion period. Set the From date and time and the To date and time. Add as many periods as needed — each one is a separate date range within the same calendar.

Click Create Calendar when done.

New calendar — April Weekends with four weekend exclusion periods defined

Once a calendar is created, assign it to a schedule by editing the schedule and selecting the calendar in the Select a maintenance calendar field. The Process Monitor will automatically skip runs during any excluded periods.

8
Step 8

Create the Process Monitor

The Process Monitor ties everything together — your scenario, rWatcher, and schedule. This is the final configuration step before monitoring goes live.

Before you continue — confirm you have
  • Salesforce login scenario visible in Director under Projects
  • rWatcher-Salesforce-01 showing green available icon
  • 5 mins schedule created and active
1

Click Process Monitors in the top navigation → click + Add process monitor.

Process Monitors page — click Add process monitor
2

Fill in the form:

Section 1 — Process monitor information:

  • Process monitor name — enter Salesforce Login Check
  • Project — select Test
  • Process — select Salesforce login
  • Select an execution schedule — select 5 mins

Section 2 — rWatcher Distribution and Locations:

  • Select distribution type — leave as Selected rWatcher
  • Select locations — select rWatcher-Salesforce-01
Process Monitor fully configured — all sections filled in
3

Scroll down to Section 3 — Process monitor events. Events are alert rules that notify you when something goes wrong with your monitored scenario. Click + New event.

Section 3 — Process monitor events, click + New event

The Set threshold and event dialog appears. For a basic Salesforce login monitor, set up an Error event so you are notified whenever the login fails:

  • Select monitoring item — select Salesforce login
  • Continuously Notify — check this to keep receiving alerts while the failure persists
  • When response time — change to Error (triggers on any failure, no threshold needed)
  • Where — leave as any location
  • Event level — set to warning or critical
  • Do — select Email and enter your email address
Set threshold and event — Error event configured for Salesforce login with email notification

The message body is pre-filled with a template. You can customize it using the available parameters shown in the blue panel — such as {bot}, {name}, {step}, and {timelinelink} to include dynamic information in your alert email.

Event message — customizable template with dynamic parameters

Click Save to add the event.

4

Click Publish in the top right corner. When prompted, click YES to start monitoring immediately.

5

You return to the Process Monitors list. Your monitor appears with a blue active toggle, showing project, schedule, and rWatcher all correctly assigned.

Process Monitor created and active — monitoring Salesforce login every 5 minutes
9
Step 9

See it run

Your Process Monitor is now live. Within 5 minutes the bot will automatically open a browser, navigate to Salesforce, log in, and log out — exactly as you recorded it.

Confirm the run started

Go to Schedules → Queue. You will see your monitor listed with a running status and a start time — triggered automatically by the Scheduler.

Queue tab — process monitor running, triggered automatically by Scheduler

Watch the bot in action

Click the monitor icon in Director to open rDesktop Manager. You can watch your rWatcher's live desktop session — it has its own Windows desktop and browser, executing your Salesforce scenario in real time.

rDesktopManager — rWatcher01 logged into Salesforce, executing the scenario live

Confirm runs completed

After the first run completes, return to Schedules → Queue. You will see completed runs with checkmarks, start and end times, and Requested by: Scheduler — confirming the automation triggered itself.

Queue — completed runs triggered automatically by the Scheduler

Check the Dashboard

Click Dashboard in the top navigation to see your process monitor results — number of successful and failed checks, average response time, and a trend line over time.

Dashboard — PM01 showing 100% success across 4 checks, average response 10.41 sec

Troubleshooting — accessing log files

Where to find log files when something goes wrong

Log files record every action, timestamp, and error in detail. When troubleshooting a problem that is not obvious from the UI, check these locations:

Director logs — C:\Program Files\Automai\Director\logs
Key files: Director_logs.log, Process_report.log, db.log

BotManager logs — C:\Program Files\Automai\BotManager\logs
Key files: BotManager.log, rDesktopManager-Watchers.log, rUserManager.log
Individual bot logs are in the Bots subfolder — e.g. rWatcher001_sc.log

ScenarioBuilder logs — C:\Documents
Key files: awplayer.log, ScenarioBuilder_Exceptions.log

→ Full article: How to access Automai log files

10
Step 10

Export reports

Once your Process Monitor has been running for a while, you can export detailed monitoring data from the Reports tab. Automai Watcher provides three report types, all exported as CSV files that you can open in Excel or any spreadsheet tool.

Summary report
High-level performance data across all runs — process name, elapsed time, variation from reference time, and error messages.
Raw data
Detailed execution data for each run including rWatcher, BotManager, start and end times, and elapsed time per iteration.
Transaction data
Same as raw data but broken down at the transaction level — useful when your scenario has multiple steps you want to measure individually.

Create a report

1

Click Reports in the top navigation. The Manage reports page appears with three buttons: + Summary report, + Raw data, and + Transaction data.

Reports tab — empty, three report type buttons visible
2

Click the report type you want. Each opens a configuration dialog. For all three, you need to:

  • Select process monitors — choose PM01
  • Report name — give it a descriptive name
  • File name — the CSV file name prefix
  • Report output full path — optional, where to save the file on the server
  • Duration / Time interval — choose the time range (Previous day, Last week, Yesterday etc.)
  • Email address — to receive the report automatically on a schedule

Check I want to create and email reports with custom intervals to schedule automatic delivery.

3

Summary report — select PM01, name it, set Time Interval to Last week, enter your email and check the custom intervals box. Click Create.

Create summary report — PM01 selected, Last week interval, email delivery enabled
4

Raw data — select PM01, name it, set Duration to Previous day. Click Create.

Create raw data report — PM01 selected, Previous day duration
5

Transaction data — select PM01, name it, set Time Interval to Yesterday, check Include column names. Click Create.

Create transaction raw data — PM01 selected, Yesterday interval, column names included
6

All three reports now appear in the Manage reports list showing their type, name, process monitor, email, interval, and timezone.

Manage reports — three reports created for PM01
7

To generate a report immediately, click the Play button (▶) next to the report. Go to Schedules → Queue to confirm the report is being generated — it appears with a processing or completed status.

Queue — reports being generated, Raw Data 01 and Transaction Data 01 completed

Once completed, click the Download icon (↓) next to the report to download the CSV file.

What to look for in the data

Pay attention to the Variation column in summary reports — it shows the percentage difference from the reference time for each run. A large variation indicates a performance change worth investigating. The Error message column shows exactly what failed and when.

What you have accomplished

By completing this guide, you now have the following set up and running in your environment:

  • Automai Watcher installed and running on your server
  • BotManager connected and online
  • A Salesforce login scenario recorded, validated, and stored in Director
  • rWatcher-Salesforce-01 active and available on BotManager-1
  • A 5-minute schedule configured and active
  • A Process Monitor running automatically every 5 minutes
  • A live Dashboard showing real-time monitoring results
  • A Summary report, Raw data report, and Transaction data report configured for PM01 and scheduled for automatic email delivery

This guide used Salesforce login as the example — but you can apply the same steps to monitor any application. Record a scenario for your own app, assign it to an rWatcher, and Automai will monitor it the same way.

Still need help? Report a problem and our support team will get back to you.
